findings.title: Superadmin operations gated only by isAdmin, never isMegaAdmin (privilege escalation)
findings.description: create-user, delete-user, modify-password-admin/{user_id}, all-lists and toggle-list check only payload.get('isAdmin'). The frontend restricts these to isMegaAdmin (or username=='Mathieu'), but the backend never enforces isMegaAdmin. The intended admin/superadmin trust boundary does not exist server-side.
findings.exploit_scenario: A non-mega admin authenticates, then calls PATCH /api/modify-password-admin/{megaadmin_user_id} with a password of their choosing. The endpoint accepts it (isAdmin true) and sets the superadmin's password + firstConnection=true. The attacker logs in as the superadmin. Full account takeover / vertical privilege escalation.
findings.impact: Any admin can take over any other account (including the superadmin), create/delete users, and toggle any list. The megaAdmin privilege level is security theater.
findings.recommendation: Add a server-side isMegaAdmin check (e.g. a require_megaadmin dependency) on create-user, delete-user, modify-password-admin, all-lists and toggle-list. For object-level operations, also scope by ownership.
findings.verification: self-verified (code traced; grep confirms no isMegaAdmin enforcement in backend)

findings.title: SSRF via unvalidated image URL fetch
findings.description: get_image(url, name) performs requests.get(url) on an admin-supplied URL with no scheme/host allowlist, no timeout, and no content-type/size validation. Reached from add-item and modify-item via the 'image' field.
findings.exploit_scenario: An admin submits an item whose image URL is http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ or an internal-only service URL. The server fetches it. Blind SSRF (body written to a file rather than returned), but internal endpoints can be probed and reached from the server's network position.
findings.impact: Server-side requests to internal/cloud-metadata endpoints from an admin-controlled value. Reduced severity because it is admin-gated.
findings.recommendation: Validate the URL: require https scheme, resolve and block private/link-local/loopback IP ranges, set a timeout, cap response size, and verify content-type is an image.
findings.verification: self-verified (code traced: url -> requests.get with no validation)

findings.title: No object-level ownership check on modify/delete item
findings.description: modify-item and delete-item verify isAdmin but not that the idea belongs to the calling admin's list. Any admin can modify or delete any other admin's items.
findings.exploit_scenario: Admin A calls PUT /api/modify-item/ or DELETE /api/delete-item/{id} with the id of an item on admin B's list. The change is applied with no ownership check.
findings.impact: Horizontal privilege escalation between admins over gift items. Low practical impact given a small trusted admin set.
findings.recommendation: Scope item mutations to items whose list belongs to the calling user (or restrict cross-list edits to megaAdmin).

findings.title: Production DB credentials default to admin/admin when env unset
findings.description: In production mode, DATABASE_USER/DATABASE_PASSWORD fall back to 'admin'/'admin' if the env vars are not set. Unlike SECRET_KEY (which refuses weak values in prod), the DB credentials have no such guard.
findings.exploit_scenario: A production deploy that forgets to set DATABASE_PASSWORD silently runs with admin/admin, which an attacker on the DB network can guess.
findings.impact: Weak default DB credentials in production if env is misconfigured.
findings.recommendation: Apply the same fail-fast guard used for SECRET_KEY: refuse to start in production if DATABASE_PASSWORD is unset or weak.

appendix_below_gate: untake-api lets any authenticated user release another user's reservation (IDOR, ~6/10, low impact)
No rate limiting / account lockout on /api/login (A04; rate-limiting is hard-excluded but noted as design rec)
dependabot/fetch-metadata@v3 is not SHA-pinned (third-party action; pull_request_target workflow does not checkout PR code, so low risk)
